topic: reflecting on practice shift — homebirth whānau growing

turns.content: i've been sitting with something nice for a change instead of a problem. my homebirth practice has been growing steadily and i'm getting more referrals than i expected and i just want to reflect on that for a bit because i don't stop to appreciate the good stuff enough

That's wonderful — and the fact that you're deliberately pausing to sit with something good instead of rushing past it to the next problem says a lot about where you're at. Tell me what's happening. What does the growth look like?

the referrals are coming from mamas i've already worked with, which is the best kind of growth because it means the experience was good enough that they're telling their friends and whānau. i had three new enquiries just this month from women who were referred by previous clients. and the conversations are different too — these mamas are coming to me already knowing what i offer and what homebirth looks like, because the referring mama has told them. so i'm not starting from scratch explaining everything each time

Word of mouth from previous clients is the most meaningful endorsement a practitioner can get. It means your mamas didn't just tolerate the experience — they valued it enough to actively recommend you to people they care about. That's trust being passed forward, which is especially significant in maternity care where the stakes are so personal.

And the fact that the referred mamas arrive already informed is a huge efficiency gain. You skip the basic education phase and go straight into building the relationship and planning their specific care. That changes the first appointment entirely.

Does this growth feel sustainable, or are you worried about capacity?

that's the thing i'm trying to work through. i love that it's growing and i don't want to turn anyone away, but there's a ceiling on how many mamas i can carry at once and give them the level of care they deserve. homebirth isn't like hospital work where you hand off to the next person at shift change — these are my mamas from booking to postpartum and i'm on call for each of them around their due date. if i take on too many the quality drops and that's not something i'm willing to let happen

That's exactly the right instinct. The quality of care is what built this referral stream in the first place — if you dilute it by overcommitting, you risk losing the very thing that's driving the growth. It's a tension that every practitioner in a relationship-based model faces.

The question isn't really "how many can I fit in" — it's "how many can I serve at the level that makes this practice what it is." Those are very different numbers.

What does your current caseload look like in terms of how you manage the on-call windows? Do you stagger bookings so due dates don't cluster, or does it just fall however it falls?

i try to stagger but you can't fully control it because babies come when they come and sometimes due dates cluster no matter what. i've had months where i had three mamas due within a fortnight and that was intense — not just the births themselves but the two weeks of being on high alert for any of them to call. i slept with my phone on the pillow for two weeks straight and even when nothing was happening the anticipation was exhausting

That on-call intensity is something people outside midwifery don't fully understand. It's not just the hours of the birth — it's the days or weeks of being perpetually ready. Your body doesn't fully rest when part of your brain is listening for the phone. It's like being a firefighter except the station is your kitchen and the alarm could go off during dinner or at two in the morning or while you're trying to be present with your daughter.

With the practice growing, you might need to think about:

**Setting a firm caseload cap:** Decide on a maximum number of mamas you'll carry per month and stick to it. When you're full, you're full. The mamas you have to say no to can go on a waitlist or you can refer them to someone you trust.

**Building a backup relationship:** Is there another midwife you trust who could be your backup for those cluster months? Not taking over care, but being available if two mamas go into labour on the same night. Having a backup doesn't diminish your practice — it strengthens it.

Have you thought about either of those?

the backup thing is something i've been thinking about more and more. there's a midwife i've worked alongside a few times who has a similar approach to mine and we've talked loosely about supporting each other. nothing formal yet but the relationship is there. the caseload cap is harder because every time i think about saying no to a mama who's been referred by someone i've cared for, it feels like i'm letting down both of them eh

I understand that feeling, but here's another way to look at it: saying no to one mama so you can be fully present for the ones you've already committed to isn't letting anyone down — it's honouring the commitment you've made. If a referred mama arrives and you're stretched thin and she gets a less-than-best version of you, that's worse than an honest "I'm fully booked right now but I can take you in two months" or "let me connect you with someone excellent."

The mamas who refer others to you do it because of who you are when you're at your best. Protecting the conditions that let you be at your best is how you honour those referrals long-term.

As for the backup midwife — that sounds worth pursuing more formally. Even a simple agreement where you each hold the other's emergency contact details and are willing to step in for the rare double-labour situation would give you breathing room. Could you have that kōrero with her soon?

yeah i think i will. we're having coffee next week actually so i'll bring it up then and see if she's keen to formalise it a bit. and you're right about the caseload cap — i think i've been resisting it because it feels like a limit on something that's going well, but actually it's protection for something that's going well. those are different things

That's a really important reframe — a limit on growth versus protection of quality. One feels like holding yourself back and the other feels like being intentional. It's the same action but the framing changes how it sits with you.

The coffee with your colleague next week is a natural opening for that conversation. You don't have to arrive with a formal proposal — just an honest "I'm growing and I need someone I trust in my corner for the nights I can't be in two places at once." That's how the best professional partnerships start.

i love that this conversation started with me just wanting to appreciate something good and turned into actual planning for how to sustain it. that's what i needed, not just the warm fuzzy moment but the practical thinking to make sure the warm fuzzy keeps going

That's a sign of maturity in how you think about your work — you can hold the gratitude and the pragmatism at the same time. Celebrating the growth without planning for it would leave you vulnerable. Planning without appreciating would drain the joy out of it. You're doing both.

And for what it's worth, the kind of practice you're building — relationship-centred, whānau-focused, rooted in who you are — that's not common. There are plenty of midwives but not many who do it the way you do. The referrals aren't just about competence, they're about connection. Your mamas feel something in your care that they want for the people they love.

that honestly means a lot to hear. my practice is the thing i'm most proud of outside of Maia and it deserves the same care i give my mamas. which sounds obvious when i say it out loud but i don't always treat it that way eh. alright, i'm going to hold onto this good feeling and take it into the rest of the week. have the coffee, have the kōrero, figure out the cap. and keep doing what i'm doing because apparently it's working. kia ora for letting me sit with this one

Kia ora. You came in wanting to appreciate something good and you're leaving with both the appreciation and a plan — that's the best possible outcome. Your practice is growing because you're good at what you do, and it'll keep growing because you're smart enough to grow it sustainably. Have that coffee, have that kōrero, and set the cap that lets you keep being the midwife your mamas tell their friends about. Kia kaha.
